title: Improved deterministic l2 robustness on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## IMPROVED DETERMINISTIC _l_ 2 ROBUSTNESS ON CIFAR-10 AND CIFAR-100
**Sahil Singla**[1] **, Surbhi Singla**[2] **, Soheil Feizi**[1] University of Maryland, College Park {ssingla,sfeizi}@umd.edu[1] , surbhisingla1995@gmail.com[2]

## ABSTRACT

Training convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with a strict Lipschitz constraint under the _l_ 2 norm is useful for provable adversarial robustness, interpretable gradients and stable training. While 1-Lipschitz CNNs can be designed by enforcing a 1-Lipschitz constraint on each layer, training such networks requires each layer to have an orthogonal Jacobian matrix (for all inputs) to prevent the gradients from vanishing during backpropagation. A layer with this property is said to be Gradient Norm Preserving (GNP). In this work, we introduce a procedure to certify the robustness of 1-Lipschitz CNNs by relaxing the orthogonalization of the last linear layer of the network that significantly advances the state of the art for both standard and provable robust accuracies on CIFAR-100 (gains of 4 _._ 80% and 4 _._ 71%, respectively). We further boost their robustness by introducing (i) a novel Gradient Norm preserving activation function called the Householder activation function (that includes every GroupSort activation) and (ii) a certificate regularization. On CIFAR-10, we achieve significant improvements over prior works in provable robust accuracy (5 _._ 81%) with only a minor drop in standard accuracy ( _−_ 0 _._ 29%). Code for reproducing all experiments in the paper is available at https://github.com/singlasahil14/SOC.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Given a neural network _f_ : R _[d] →_ R _[k]_ , the Lipschitz constant[1] Lip( _f_ ) enforces an upper bound on how much the output is allowed to change in proportion to a change in the input. Previous work has demonstrated that a small Lipschitz constant is useful for improved adversarial robustness (Szegedy et al., 2014; Cissé et al., 2017), generalization bounds (Bartlett et al., 2017; Long & Sedghi, 2020), interpretable gradients (Tsipras et al., 2018) and Wasserstein distance estimation (Villani, 2008). Lip( _f_ ) also upper bounds the increase in the norm of gradient during backpropagation and can thus prevent gradient explosion during training, enabling us to train very deep networks (Xiao et al., 2018). While heuristic methods to enforce Lipschitz constraints (Miyato et al., 2018; Gulrajani et al., 2017) have achieved much practical success, they do not provably enforce a bound on Lip( _f_ ) globally and it remains challenging to achieve similar results when Lip( _f_ ) is provably bounded.

Using the property: Lip( _g ◦ h_ ) _≤_ Lip( _g_ ) Lip( _h_ ), the Lipschitz constant of the neural network can be bounded by the product of the Lipschitz constant of all layers. While this allows us to construct 1-Lipschitz neural networks by constraining each layer to be 1-Lipschitz, Anil et al. (2018) identified a key difficulty with this approach. Because a 1-Lipschitz layer can only reduce the norm of gradient during backpropagation, backprop through each layer reduces the gradient norm, resulting in small gradient values for layers closer to the input, making training slow and difficult. To address this problem, they introduce Gradient Norm Preserving (GNP) architectures where each layer preserves the gradient norm during backpropagation. This involves constraining the Jacobian of each linear layer to be an orthogonal matrix and using a GNP activation function called GroupSort. GroupSort activation function (Anil et al., 2018) first separates the vector of preactivations **z** _∈_ R _[m]_ into groups of pre-specified sizes, sorts each group in the descending order and then concatenates these sorted groups. When the group size is 2, the resulting activation function is called MaxMin.

> 1Unless specified, we assume the Lipschitz constant under the _l_ 2 norm in this work.

For 1-Lipschitz CNNs, the robustness certificate for a sample **x** from class _l_ is computed as _Mf_ ( **x** ) _/√_ 2 where _Mf_ ( **x** ) = _fl_ ( **x** ) _−_ max _i_ = _l fi_ ( **x** ). Naturally, larger values of _fl_ ( **x** ) and smaller values of max _j_ = _l fj_ ( **x** ) will lead to larger certificates. This requires the last weight matrix in the network, denoted by **W** _∈_ R _[k][×][m]_ ( _k_ is the number of classes, _m_ is the dimension of the penultimate layer, _m > k_ ), to enforce the following constraints throughout training:

where **W** _i,_ : denotes the _i[th]_ row of **W** . Now suppose that for some input image with label _l_ , we want to update **W** to increase the logit for the _l[th]_ class. Since _∥_ **W** _l,_ : _∥_ 2 is constrained to be 1, the logit can only be increased by changing the direction of the vector **W** _l,_ :. Because the other rows _{_ **W** _i,_ : _, i ̸_ = _l}_ are constrained to be orthogonal to **W** _l,_ :, this further requires an update for all the rows of **W** . Thus during training, any update made to learn some class must necessitate the forgetting of information relevant for the other classes. This can be particularly problematic when the number of classes _k_ and thus the number of orthogonality constraints per row (i.e., _k −_ 1) is large (such as in CIFAR-100).

To address this limitation, we propose to keep the last weight layer of the network _unchanged._ But then the resulting function is no longer 1-Lipschitz and the certificate _Mf_ ( **x** ) _/√_ 2 is not valid. Thus, we introduce a new certification procedure that does not require the last weight layer of the network **W** to be orthogonal. Our certificate is then given by the following equation:

However, a limitation of using the above certificate is that because the weight layers are completely unconstrained, larger norms of rows (i.e., _∥_ **W** _i,_ : _∥_ ) can result in larger values of _∥_ **W** _l,_ : _−_ **W** _i,_ : _∥_ 2 and thus smaller certificate values. To address this limitation, we normalize all rows to be of unit norm before computing the logits. While this still requires all the rows to be of unit norm, their directions are now allowed to change freely thus preventing the need to update other rows and forgetting of learned information. We show that this provides significant improvements when the number of classes is large. We call this procedure _Last Layer Normalization_ (abbreviated as _LLN_ ). On CIFAR-100, this significantly improves both the standard ( _>_ 3%) and provable robust accuracy ( _>_ 4% at _ρ_ = 36 _/_ 255) across multiple 1-Lipschitz CNN architectures (Table 1). Here, _ρ_ is the _l_ 2 attack radius.

Another limitation of existing 1-Lipschitz CNNs (Li et al., 2019b; Trockman & Kolter, 2021; Singla & Feizi, 2021) is that their robustness guarantees do not scale properly with the _l_ 2 radius _ρ_ . For example, the provable robust accuracy of (Singla & Feizi, 2021) drops _∼_ 30% at _ρ_ = 108 _/_ 255 compared to 36 _/_ 255 on CIFAR-10 (Table 2). To address this limitation, we introduce a certificate regularization denoted by CR (Section 5) that when used along with the Householder activation results in significantly improved provable robust accuracy at larger radius values with minimal loss in standard accuracy. On the CIFAR-10 dataset, we achieve significant improvements in the provable robust accuracy for large _ρ_ = 108 _/_ 255 (min gain of +4 _._ 96%) across different architectures with minimal loss in the standard accuracy (max drop of _−_ 0 _._ 56%). Results are in Table 2.

Additionally, we characterize the MaxMin activation function as a special case of the more general _Householder_ (HH) activations. Recall that given **z** _∈_ R _[m]_ , the HH transformation is a linear function reflecting **z** about the hyperplane **v** _[T]_ **x** = 0 ( _∥_ **v** _∥_ 2 = 1), given by ( **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ ) **z** where **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ is orthogonal because _∥_ **v** _∥_ 2 = 1. The _Householder_ activation function _σ_ **v** is defined below:

First, note that since **z** = ( **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ ) **z** along **v** _[T]_ **z** = 0, _σ_ **v** is continuous. Moreover, the Jacobian _∇_ **z** _σ_ **v** is either **I** or **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ (both orthogonal) implying _σ_ **v** is GNP. Since these properties hold _∀_ **v** : _∥_ **v** _∥_ 2 = 1, **v** can be learned during the training. In fact, we prove that any GNP piecewise linear function that changes from **Q** 1 **z** to **Q** 2 **z** ( **Q** 1 _,_ **Q** 2 are square orthogonal matrices) along **v** _[T]_ **z** = 0 must satisfy **Q** 2 = **Q** 1 � **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_[ �] to be continuous (Theorem 1). Thus, this characterization proves that every GroupSort activation is a special case of the more general Householder activation function (example in Figure 1, discussion in Section 6).

In summary, in this paper, we make the following contributions:

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

- We introduce a certification procedure _without_ orthogonalizing the last linear layer called _Last Layer Normalization_ . This procedure significantly enhances the standard and provable robust accuracy when the number of classes is large. Using the LipConvnet-15 network on CIFAR-100, our modification achieves a gain of +4 _._ 71% in provable robust accuracy (at _ρ_ = 36 _/_ 255) with a gain of +4 _._ 80% in standard accuracy (Table 1).

- We introduce a _Certificate Regularizer_ that significantly advances the provable robust accuracy with a small reduction in standard accuracy. Using LipConvnet-15 network on CIFAR-10, we achieve +5 _._ 81% improvement in provable robust accuracy (at _ρ_ = 108 _/_ 255) with only a _−_ 0 _._ 29% drop in standard accuracy over the existing methods (Table 2).

- We introduce a class of piecewise linear GNP activation functions called _Householder_ or HH activations. We show that the MaxMin activation is a special case of the HH activation for certain settings. We prove that Householder transformations are _necessary_ for any GNP piecewise linear function to be continuous (Theorem 1).

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Provably Lipschitz convolutional neural networks** : The class of fully connected neural networks (FCNs) which are Gradient Norm Preserving (GNP) and 1-Lipschitz were first introduced by Anil et al. (2018). They orthogonalize weight matrices and use GroupSort as the activation function to design each layer to be GNP. While there have been numerous works on enforcing Lipschitz constraints on convolution layers (Cissé et al., 2017; Tsuzuku et al., 2018; Qian & Wegman, 2019; Gouk et al., 2020; Sedghi et al., 2019), they either enforce loose Lipschitz bounds or are not scalable to large networks. To ensure that the Lipschitz constraint on convolutional layers is tight, multiple recent works try to construct convolution layers with an orthogonal Jacobian matrix (Li et al., 2019b; Trockman & Kolter, 2021; Singla & Feizi, 2021). These approaches avoid the aforementioned issues and allow the training of large, provably 1-Lipschitz CNNs while achieving impressive results.

**Provable defenses against adversarial examples** : A provably robust classifier is one for which we can guarantee that the classifier’s prediction remains constant within some region around the input. Most of the existing methods for provable robustness either bound the Lipschitz constant of the neural network or the individual layers (Weng et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019; 2018; Wong et al., 2018; Wong & Kolter, 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018; Croce et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2018; Singla & Feizi, 2020; Zhang et al., 2021; 2022; Wang et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021). However, these methods do not scale to large and practical networks on the ImageNet dataset (Deng et al., 2009). To scale to such large networks, randomized smoothing (Liu et al., 2018; Cao & Gong, 2017; Lécuyer et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019a; Cohen et al., 2019; Salman et al., 2019; Levine et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020a;b) has been proposed as a _probabilistically certified defense_ . However, certifying robustness with high probability requires generating a large number of noisy samples leading to high inference-time computation. In contrast, the defense we propose is deterministic and hence not comparable to randomized smoothing. While Levine & Feizi (2021) provide deterministic robustness certificates using randomized smoothing, their certificates are in the _l_ 1 norm and not directly applicable for the _l_ 2 threat model studied in this work. We discuss the differences between _l_ 1 and _l_ 2 certificates in Appendix Section C.

## 4 LAST LAYER NORMALIZATION

To ensure that the network is 1-Lipschitz so that the certificate in equation (3) is valid, existing 1-Lipschitz neural networks require the weight matrices of all the linear layers of the network to be orthogonal. For the weight matrix in the last layer of the network (that maps the penultimate layer neurons to the logits), **W** _∈_ R _[k][×][m]_ ( _k_ is the number of classes, _m_ is the dimension of the penultimate layer, _m > k_ ), this enforces the following constraints on each row **W** _i,_ : _∈_ R _[m]_ :

_∀j, ∥_ **W** _j,_ : _∥_ 2 = 1 _, ∀i ̸_ = _j,_ **W** _j,_ : _⊥_ **W** _i,_ : (4)

Now suppose that for some input **x** with label _l_ , we want to update **W** to increase the logit for the _l[th]_ class. Since _∥_ **W** _l,_ : _∥_ 2 is constrained to be 1, the gradient update can only change the direction of the vector **W** _l,_ :. But now, because the other rows _{_ **W** _i,_ : _, i ̸_ = _l}_ are constrained to be orthogonal to **W** _l,_ :, this further requires an update for all the rows of **W** . This has the negative effect that during training, any update made to learn some class must necessitate the forgetting of information relevant for the other classes. This can be particularly problematic when the number of classes _k_ is large (such as in CIFAR-100) and thus the number of orthogonality constraints per row i.e. _k −_ 1 is large.

To address this limitation, first observe that the neural network from the input layer to the penultimate layer (i.e excluding the last linear layer) is 1-Lipschitz. Let _g_ : R _[d] →_ R _[m]_ be this function so that _f_ ( **x** ) = **W** _g_ ( **x** ) + **b** . This equation suggests that even if **W** is not orthogonal, the Lipschitz constant of the function _fl − fi_ , can be computed by multiplying the Lipschitz constant of _g_ (which is 1) and that of ( **W** _l,_ : _−_ **W** _i,_ :) (which is _∥_ **W** _l,_ : _−_ **W** _i,_ : _∥_ 2). The robustness certificate can then be computed as _Mf_ ( **x** ) _/∥_ **W** _l,_ : _−_ **W** _i,_ : _∥_ 2. This procedure leads to the following proposition:

**Proposition 1.** _Given_ 1 _-Lipschitz continuous function g_ : R _[d] →_ R _[m] and_ **W** _∈_ R _[k][×][m] ,_ **b** _∈_ R _[k] (k is the number of classes), construct a new function f_ : R _[d] →_ R _[k] defined as: f_ ( **x** ) = **W** _g_ ( **x** ) + **b** _. Let fl_ ( **x** ) _>_ max _i_ = _l fi_ ( **x** ) _. The robustness certificate (under the l_ 2 _norm) is given by:_

This provides significant improvements when the number of classes is large. We call this procedure _Last Layer Normalization_ ( _LLN_ ).

## 5 CERTIFICATE REGULARIZATION

A limitation of using cross entropy loss for training 1-Lipschitz CNNs is that it is not explicitly designed to maximize the margin _Mf_ ( **x** ) and thus, the robustness certificate. That is, once the cross entropy loss becomes small, the gradients will no longer try to further increase the margin _Mf_ ( **x** ) even though the network may have the capacity to learn bigger margins.

In the above equation, _f_ Ω denotes the 1-Lipschitz neural network parametrized by Ω, _f_ Ω( **x** ) denotes the logits for the input **x** , _ℓ_ ( _f_ Ω( **x** ) _, l_ ) is the cross entropy loss for input **x** with label _l_ and _γ >_ 0 is the regularization coefficient for maximizing the certificate. We have the minus sign in front of the regularization term _γ_ relu( _Mf_ ( **x** ) _/√_ 2) because we want to _maximize_ the certificate while _minimizing_ the cross entropy loss. For wrongly classified inputs, _Mf_ ( **x** ) _/√_ 2 _<_ 0 = _⇒_ relu( _Mf_ ( **x** ) _/√_ 2) = 0. This ensures that the optimization tries to increase the certificates _only_ for the correctly classified inputs. We call the above mentioned procedure _Certificate Regularization_ (abbreviated as _CR_ ).

## 6 HOUSEHOLDER ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS

Recall that given **z** _∈_ R _[m]_ , the Householder (HH) transformation reflects **z** about the hyperplane **v** _[T]_ **x** = 0 where _∥_ **v** _∥_ 2 = 1. The linear transformation is given by the equation ( **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ ) **z** where **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ is orthogonal because _∥_ **v** _∥_ 2 = 1. Now, consider the nonlinear function _σ_ **v** defined below:

**Definition 1.** _(_ _**Householder Activation of Order**_ 1 _) The activation function σ_ **v** : R _[m] →_ R _[m] , applied on_ **z** _∈_ R _[m] , is called the m-dimensional Householder Activation of Order_ 1 _:_

Since _σ_ **v** is linear when **v** _[T]_ **z** _>_ 0 or **v** _[T]_ **z** _<_ 0, it is also continuous in both cases. At the hyperplane separating the two cases (i.e., **v** _[T]_ **z** = 0) we have: ( **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ ) **z** = **z** _−_ 2( **v** _[T]_ **z** ) **v** = **z** (both linear functions are equal). Thus, _σ_ **v** is continuous _∀_ **z** _∈_ R _[m]_ . Moreover, the Jacobian is either **I** or

While the above arguments suggest that HH transformations are _sufficient_ to ensure such functions are continuous, we also prove that they are _necessary_ . That is, we prove that if a GNP piecewise linear function _g_ : R _[m] →_ R _[m]_ transitions between different linear functions **Q** 1 **z** and **Q** 2 **z** (in an open set _S ⊂_ R _[m]_ ) along a hyperplane **v** _[T]_ **z** = 0 (where _∥_ **v** _∥_ 2 = 1), then _g_ is continuous in _S if and only if_ **Q** 2 = **Q** 1( **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ ). This theoretical result provides a general principle for designing piecewise linear GNP activation functions. The formal result is stated in the following Theorem:

**Theorem 1.** _Given an open set S ⊂_ R _[m] , orthogonal square matrices_ **Q** 1 = **Q** 2 _, and vector_ **v** _∈_ R _[m]_ ( _∥_ **v** _∥_ 2 = 1) _such that S ∩{_ **z** : **v** _[T]_ **z** = 0 _} ̸_ = _∅, the function g defined as follows:_

_is continuous in S if and only if_ **Q** 2 = **Q** 1( **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ ) _._

Proof of Theorem 1 is in Appendix A.2. Note that since the matrix **I** _−_ 2 **vv** _[T]_ has determinant _−_ 1, the above theorem necessitates that det( **Q** 1) = _−_ det( **Q** 2) i.e the determinant of the Jacobian must change sign whenever the Jacobian of a piecewise linear GNP activation function changes.

Recall that for the MaxMin activation function, MaxMin( _z_ 1 _, z_ 2) = ( _z_ 1 _, z_ 2) if _z_ 1 _> z_ 2 and ( _z_ 2 _, z_ 1) otherwise. Thus, the Jacobian of MaxMin for _z_ 1 _> z_ 2 case is **I** = **J**[+] (0) while for _z_ 1 _≤ z_ 2 is **J** _[−]_ ( _π/_ 2). Using Theorem 1, we can easily prove that MaxMin is a special case of the more general Householder activation functions where the Jacobian **J** _[−]_ ( _π/_ 2) is replaced with **J** _[−]_ ( _θ_ ) and the conditions _z_ 1 _> z_ 2 are replaced with _z_ 1 sin( _θ/_ 2) _> z_ 2 cos( _θ/_ 2) (similarly for _≤_ ). The construction of Householder activations in 2 dimensions, denoted by _σθ_ , is given in the following corollary:

**Corollary 1.** _The function σθ_ : R[2] _→_ R[2] _defined as_

_is continuous and is called_ 2D _Householder Activation of Order_ 1 _._

The two cases are demonstrated in Figure 1a and Figure 1b, respectively. Since _σθ_ is continuous, GNP and 1-Lipschitz _∀ θ ∈_ R _, θ_ is a learnable parameter. For _θ_ = _π/_ 2 in equation (8), _σθ_ is equivalent to MaxMin. Thus, _σθ_ is at least as expressive as MaxMin.

A major limitation of using _σ_ **v** (equation (6)) directly is that it has only 2 linear regions and is thus limited in its expressive power. In contrast, MaxMin first divides the preactivation **z** _∈_ R _[m]_ (assuming _m_ is divisible by 2) into _m/_ 2 groups of size 2 each. Since each group has 2 linear regions, we get 2 _[m/]_[2] linear regions from the _m/_ 2 groups. Thus, to increase the expressive power, we similarly divide **z** into _m/_ 2 groups of size 2 each and apply the 2-dimensional Householder activation function of Order 1 ( _σθ_ ) to each group resulting in 2 _[m/]_[2] linear regions (same as MaxMin).

To apply _σθ_ to the output of a convolution layer **z** _∈_ R _[m][×][n][×][n]_ ( _m_ is the number of channels and _n × n_ is the spatial size), we first split **z** into 2 tensors along the channel dimension giving the tensors: **z** : _m/_ 2 _,_ : _,_ : and **z** _m/_ 2: _,_ : _,_ :. Each of these tensors is of size _m/_ 2 _× n × n_ giving _n_[2] _m/_ 2 groups. We use the same _θ_ for each pair of channels (irrespective of spatial location) resulting in _m/_ 2 learnable parameters. We initialize each _θ_ = _π/_ 2 so that _σθ_ is equivalent to MaxMin at initialization.

## 7 EXPERIMENTS

Our goal is to evaluate the effectiveness of the three changes proposed in this work: (a) Last Layer Normalization, (b) Certificate regularization and (c) Householder activation functions. We perform experiments under the setting of provably robust image classification on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets using the same 1-Lipschitz CNN architectures used by Singla & Feizi (2021) (LipConvnet-5, 10, 15, _. . ._ , 40) due to their superior performance over the prior works. We compare with the three

6

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

orthogonal convolution layers in the literature: SOC (Singla & Feizi, 2021), BCOP (Li et al., 2019b) and Cayley (Trockman & Kolter, 2021) using MaxMin as the activation function.

We use SOC with MaxMin as the primary baseline for comparison in the maintext due to their superior performance over the prior works (BCOP, Cayley). Results using BCOP and Cayley convolutions are given in Appendix Sections H and I for completeness. We use the same implementations for these convolution layers as given in their respective github repositories. We compare the provable robust accuracy using 3 different values of the _l_ 2 perturbation radius _ρ_ = 36 _/_ 255 _,_ 72 _/_ 255 _,_ 108 _/_ 255. In both Tables 1 and 2, for all networks, we use SOC as the convolution layer. Using SOC, we achieve the same bound on the approximation error of an orthogonal Jacobian as achieved in Singla & Feizi (2021) i.e. 2 _._ 42 _×_ 10 _[−]_[6] across all convolution layers in all networks. Thus, even for the network with 40 layers, this results in maximum Lipschitz constant of (1 + 2 _._ 42 _×_ 10 _[−]_[6] )[40] = 1 _._ 000097 _≤_ 1 _._ 0001. Thus, the Lipschitz constant across all our networks is bounded by 1 _._ 0001. The symbol HH (in Tables 1, 2) is for the 2D Householder Activation of order 1 or _σθ_ (defined in equation (8)).

All experiments were performed using 1 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 Ti GPU. All networks were trained for 200 epochs with initial learning rate of 0.1, dropped by a factor of 0.1 after 100 and 150 epochs. For Certificate Regularization (or CR), we set the parameter _γ_ = 0 _._ 5.

## 7.1 RESULTS ON CIFAR-100

In Table 1, for each architecture, the row "SOC + MaxMin" uses the MaxMin activation, "+ LLN" adds Last Layer Normalization (uses MaxMin), "+ HH" replaces MaxMin with _σθ_ (also uses LLN) , "+ CR" also adds Certificate Regularization with _γ_ = 0 _._ 1 (uses both _σθ_ and LLN). The column, "Increase (Standard)" denotes the increase in standard accuracy relative to "SOC + MaxMin".

By adding LLN (the row "+ LLN"), we observe gains in standard (min gain of 1 _._ 10%) and provable robust accuracy (min gain of 1 _._ 71% at _ρ_ = 36 _/_ 255) across all the LipConvnet architectures (gains relative to "SOC + MaxMin"). These gains are smallest for the LipConvnet-40 network with the maximum depth. However, replacing MaxMin with the _σθ_ activation further improves the standard (min gain of 3 _._ 65%) and provable robust accuracy (min gain of 4 _._ 46% at _ρ_ = 36 _/_ 255) across all networks (again relative to "SOC + MaxMin"). We observe that replacing MaxMin with _σθ_ significantly improves the performance of the deeper LipConvnet-35 _,_ 40 networks.

Adding CR further improves the provable robust accuracy while only slightly reducing the standard accuracy. Because LLN significantly improves the standard accuracy, we compare the standard accuracy numbers between rows "+ CR" and "+ LLN" to evaluate the drop due to CR. We observe a small drop in standard accuracy ( _−_ 0 _._ 04% _, −_ 0 _._ 11%) only for LipConvnet-5 and LipConvnet-15 networks. For the other networks, the standard accuracy actually _increases_ .

## 7.2 RESULTS ON CIFAR-10

In Table 2, for each architecture, the row "SOC + MaxMin" uses the MaxMin activation, the row "+ HH" uses _σθ_ activation (replacing MaxMin) and the row "+ CR" also adds Certificate Regularization with _γ_ = 0 _._ 1 (again using _σθ_ as the activation). Due to the small number of classes in CIFAR-10, we do not observe significant gains using Last Layer Normalization or LLN (Appendix Table 10). Thus, we do not include LLN for any of the results in Table 2. The column, "Increase (108 _/_ 255)" denotes the increase in provable robust accuracy with _ρ_ = 108 _/_ 255 relative to "SOC + MaxMin".

For LipConvnet-25 _,_ 30 _,_ 35 _,_ 40 architectures, we observe gains in both the standard and provable robust accuracy by replacing MaxMin with the HH activation (i.e _σθ_ ). Similar to what we observe for CIFAR-100 in Table 1, the gains in provable robust accuracy ( _ρ_ = 108 _/_ 255) are significantly higher for deeper networks: LipConvnet-35 (3 _._ 65%) and LipConvnet-40 (4 _._ 35%) with decent gains in standard accuracy (1 _._ 71 and 1 _._ 61% respectively).

Again similar to CIFAR-100, adding CR further boosts the provable robust accuracy while slightly reducing the standard accuracy. Comparing "+ CR" with "SOC + MaxMin", we observe small drops in standard accuracy for LipConvnet-5 _,_ 10 _, . . . ,_ 30 networks (max. drop of _−_ 0 _._ 56%), and gains for LipConvnet-35 (+0 _._ 52%) and LipConvnet-40 (+0 _._ 96%). For provable robust accuracy ( _ρ_ = 108 _/_ 255), we observe very significant gains of _>_ 4 _._ 96% for all networks and _>_ 8% for the deeper LipConvnet-35, 40 networks.
Table 1: Results for provable robustness against adversarial examples on the CIFAR-100 dataset. Results with BCOP and Cayley convolutions are in Appendix Tables 13 and 14.

## 8 CONCLUSION

In this work, we introduce a procedure to certify robustness of 1-Lipschitz convolutional neural networks without orthogonalizing of the last linear layer of the network. Additionally, we introduce a certificate regularization that significantly improves the provable robust accuracy for these models at higher _l_ 2 radii. Finally, we introduce a class of GNP activation functions called Householder (or HH) activations and prove that the Jacobian of any Gradient Norm Preserving (GNP) piecewise linear function is only allowed to change via Householder transformations for the function to be continuous

which provides a general principle for designing piecewise linear GNP functions. These ideas lead to improved deterministic _ℓ_ 2 robustness certificates on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes three modifications to 1-Lipschitz CNNs for deterministic l2 robustness: Last Layer Normalization (LLN), a Certificate Regularizer (CR), and Householder (HH) activation functions. The authors demonstrate improved certified robust accuracy on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 compared to existing orthogonal convolution-based architectures.

The paper provides a clear and technically sound contribution to the field of deterministic certified robustness. The introduction of Householder activations as a generalization of GroupSort is a strong theoretical insight, and the empirical results show consistent gains across multiple network depths. The work successfully addresses the performance bottleneck associated with rigid orthogonality constraints in the final layers.

Overview issues:

- Unclear impact of Certificate Regularization on gradient masking: The Certificate Regularization (CR) defined in Equation 5 involves a ReLU-based penalty on the margin. While this encourages the network to increase the margin for correctly classified samples, it introduces a non-differentiable point at the decision boundary. There is a risk that this objective function could induce gradient masking or obfuscated gradients, where the model appears robust due to the loss landscape's geometry rather than true underlying robustness. The authors should provide empirical evidence, such as comparing the certified accuracy against a range of adaptive attacks or verifying the Lipschitz constant explicitly after training with CR to ensure the 1-Lipschitz property is maintained despite the non-smooth loss.

- Computational cost and training stability analysis: The paper introduces Householder activations and CR to improve robustness but lacks a detailed analysis of the computational overhead. While SOC convolutions are known to be expensive, the combination of these with HH activations and the extra forward/backward passes required for the CR term could significantly increase wall-clock time per epoch. Given the focus on deterministic robustness, which is often criticized for its high training cost, providing a comparison of training time versus robust accuracy gains across the architectures in Table 1 and 2 would be highly beneficial for practitioners.

- Justification for the choice of regularization coefficient: The authors set the regularization coefficient gamma to 0.1 for CR in the experiments (Section 7.1 and 7.2). However, there is no discussion regarding the sensitivity of the results to this hyperparameter. If the certified accuracy is highly dependent on this value, the reported gains might be overstated or difficult to replicate in different settings. An ablation study showing the trade-off between standard accuracy and certified robust accuracy for varying values of gamma would strengthen the claim that CR is a robust and generalizable technique.

- Gap between certified and empirical robustness: The paper focuses exclusively on certified robust accuracy using the margin-based certificate. However, the gap between this certified bound and the empirical robustness (e.g., against PGD attacks) remains large in many 1-Lipschitz models. It is unclear if the gains in certified accuracy are due to the model becoming inherently more robust or simply due to the certificate becoming tighter. Including empirical robustness numbers (e.g., AutoAttack) alongside the certified bounds would provide a more complete picture of the model's actual defense capabilities.

- Lack of empirical validation for the 1-Lipschitz property post-training: The paper introduces Certificate Regularization (CR) which modifies the loss function to include a non-smooth term. While the theoretical architecture is designed to be 1-Lipschitz, the optimization landscape with this new regularizer might lead to numerical instabilities or violations of the Lipschitz constraint during training. It is essential to compute the empirical Lipschitz constant of the trained networks—for instance, by estimating the spectral norm of the Jacobian across a test set—to verify that the 1-Lipschitz property is strictly maintained. Without this, the validity of the robustness certificate $\mathcal{M}_{f}(\mathbf{x})/\sqrt{2}$ remains an assumption rather than a proven guarantee for the trained models.

- Missing sensitivity analysis for the regularization coefficient gamma: The authors report performance gains using a fixed regularization coefficient of $\gamma=0.1$ for the Certificate Regularization (CR) term. Given that CR directly trades off between standard accuracy and margin maximization, the optimal value of $\gamma$ likely depends on the dataset and the network architecture. A brief ablation study showing the Pareto frontier of standard accuracy versus provable robust accuracy for different values of $\gamma$ (e.g., in the range [0.01, 0.5]) is necessary to demonstrate that the reported improvements are not merely the result of careful hyperparameter tuning for a single specific setting.

- Absence of empirical robustness evaluation against adaptive attacks: The paper focuses entirely on certified robustness, which relies on the validity of the margin-based bound. However, it is well-known in the literature that such models can sometimes exhibit 'certified robustness' that significantly exceeds their empirical robustness, or vice versa, due to the nature of the certificate. To confirm that the proposed architectural changes (LLN, HH, CR) improve the model's actual defensive capabilities, the authors should report empirical robustness numbers against standard adversarial attacks like PGD or AutoAttack. This comparison would clarify whether the gains are due to a tighter certificate or a genuine improvement in the model's decision boundary.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include an ablation study on the sensitivity of the regularization parameter gamma.

- Provide a brief analysis or empirical check to ensure that the Certificate Regularization does not lead to gradient obfuscation.

- Report the training time overhead for the proposed methods compared to the baseline SOC + MaxMin.

This discrepancy makes it impossible to determine which value was actually used to generate the reported results. Please verify the correct value and ensure consistency across the manuscript.

For LipConvnet-35, the table shows a difference of 3.17% (14.13% - 10.96%), not 3.65%. Please recalculate these figures against the table values.

comments.title: Inconsistency between text description and Table 2 data
comments.quote: Due to the small number of classes in CIFAR-10, we do not observe significant gains using Last Layer Normalization or LLN (Appendix Table 10). Thus, we do not include LLN for any of the results in Table 2.
comments.explanation: The text claims that LLN is excluded from Table 2 because it does not provide significant gains on CIFAR-10. However, the provided table includes rows for '+ LLN' for every architecture listed. Please reconcile this contradiction.

comments.title: Overstatement of necessity for Householder activations
comments.quote: We prove that Householder transformations are *necessary* for any GNP piecewise linear function to be continuous (Theorem 1).
comments.explanation: The claim that Householder transformations are 'necessary' for any GNP piecewise linear function to be continuous is imprecise. Theorem 1 establishes that if a function transitions between two linear operators across a hyperplane, continuity requires the transition operator to be a Householder reflection. This identifies the transition mechanism, but it does not imply that the entire activation function must be a Householder activation as defined in Equation (1). Please clarify that the necessity applies to the transition operator between linear regions.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes three modifications to 1-Lipschitz CNNs for deterministic l2 robustness: Last Layer Normalization, Certificate Regularization, and Householder activations. The theoretical motivation is sound, and the ablation structure demonstrates incremental gains. However, the experimental evaluation contains critical omissions and internal inconsistencies that must be addressed before publication.

The authors make a clear contribution by relaxing orthogonality constraints on the final layer and introducing a generalized GNP activation class. The theoretical characterization of Householder transformations as necessary for continuous piecewise linear GNP functions is a strong theoretical result. The empirical improvements on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 are promising, but the evaluation framework lacks standard robustness benchmarks and statistical rigor.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity in Last Layer Normalization Training Dynamics: Section 4 states that rows of the final weight matrix are normalized to unit norm 'before computing the logits,' but it does not clarify whether this normalization is enforced as a hard constraint during gradient updates or applied solely as a post-processing step at inference and certification time. If normalization is only applied post-hoc, the training objective optimizes an unnormalized weight matrix, which severs the link between the training dynamics and the Lipschitz bound used for certification. This creates a risk that the certified radius is either invalid or significantly looser than claimed. The authors must explicitly describe the optimization procedure for W and either project the weights onto the unit sphere after every update or provide a theoretical argument showing why post-hoc normalization preserves the certificate.

- Contradiction in Regularization Hyperparameter Reporting: The paper reports conflicting values for the certificate regularization coefficient gamma. Section 7 explicitly states, 'For Certificate Regularization (or CR), we set the parameter gamma=0.5,' whereas the captions for Tables 1 and 2 specify that CR uses gamma=0.1. This discrepancy undermines confidence in the experimental setup and reproducibility. The authors need to correct the text to match the tables (or vice versa) and clarify whether gamma was tuned per dataset or held constant across all experiments. A clear description of the hyperparameter search space and selection protocol is necessary.

- Missing Empirical Robustness Against Adaptive Attacks: The experimental evaluation in Section 7 relies exclusively on provable (certified) accuracy. Adversarial robustness research requires complementing theoretical certificates with empirical robustness evaluations against adaptive attack suites like AutoAttack or APGD. Certified accuracy provides a lower bound on robustness, but without empirical results, readers cannot assess whether the certificates are tight or if the model remains vulnerable to practical, gradient-based attacks. Adding an empirical robustness section would validate the practical utility of the proposed methods and align the paper with standard evaluation protocols in the field.

- Lack of Statistical Variance in Reported Results: Tables 1 and 2 present single point estimates for standard and provable robust accuracy across all architectures and methods. Training 1-Lipschitz networks with orthogonal or normalized weight matrices is highly sensitive to initialization and optimization paths. The claimed improvements, such as the +4.71% gain on CIFAR-100, lack standard deviations or confidence intervals, making it impossible to verify statistical significance. The authors should report results averaged over multiple random seeds and include variance metrics to substantiate the performance gains and rule out optimization noise.

- Absence of Computational Efficiency and Scalability Analysis: The introduction of LLN, certificate regularization, and Householder activations adds architectural and optimization complexity, yet the paper provides no analysis of computational overhead. Deterministic certification is inherently more expensive than standard inference, and the added regularization and activation functions may impact training throughput or memory usage. The authors should report training time, inference latency, and certification time compared to the SOC, BCOP, and Cayley baselines. This information is necessary to evaluate the practical scalability of the proposed framework and to position the method against probabilistic alternatives.

- Unjustified Parameter Sharing in Householder Activations: Section 6 explains that the Householder activation uses a single learnable parameter theta per channel pair, shared across all spatial locations. While this reduces the parameter count, it severely restricts the spatial adaptivity of the activation function compared to per-pixel or per-channel variants. The paper does not justify this design choice or analyze its impact on representational capacity. An ablation study comparing shared versus independent theta parameters would clarify whether the performance gains stem from the activation class itself or from the specific parameterization scheme, and would help readers understand the trade-off between expressivity and efficiency.

- Gradient computation through Householder activation kinks is unspecified: The paper introduces the Householder activation as a learnable component but never specifies how backpropagation handles the non-differentiable boundary at v^T z = 0. The Jacobian abruptly switches between I and I - 2vv^T. Optimization requires a clear rule for this kink, yet the text leaves it undefined. Readers cannot reproduce the reported gains or verify that the GNP property survives the update step. The authors should explicitly define the gradient rule across the boundary and justify why it preserves the Lipschitz bound during training.

- Certificate bound degenerates when class rows become collinear: The proposed certificate divides the margin by ||W_l - W_i||_2. Last Layer Normalization forces each row to unit length, which makes the denominator equal to sqrt(2(1 - cos(phi))). If training drives two class rows to become nearly parallel, the denominator approaches zero and the certificate value explodes. This yields an overconfident and mathematically invalid guarantee. The paper completely ignores this degenerate regime. Adding a minimal angular margin constraint or discussing how empirical results avoid this collapse is necessary for the certificate to be trusted in practice.

- Missing worked example for Theorem 1 and certificate reduction: Theorem 1 asserts that Householder transformations are necessary for continuity in GNP piecewise linear functions, but the proof stands alone without a concrete numerical example. Computing the Jacobian transition for a simple 2D linear classifier would help readers grasp why the determinant must flip sign. Furthermore, the new certification formula should algebraically reduce to the standard M_f(x)/sqrt(2) bound when the last-layer rows are orthogonal. Showing this reduction explicitly would anchor the theoretical contribution. It would also demonstrate that the method generalizes existing results rather than replacing them with an unrelated bound.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for Certificate Regularization coefficient: The paper claims Certificate Regularization significantly boosts provable robust accuracy at larger radii, yet it only reports results for a single fixed value of gamma. The relationship between gamma and the certified radius is never explored. A sensitivity analysis plotting robust accuracy against gamma across different network depths would clarify whether the gains are stable or require careful tuning. This analysis is essential to establish CR as a reliable component rather than a fragile hyperparameter fix. Readers will wonder if the improvements scale gracefully or vanish with minor tuning changes.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents a theoretically motivated improvement to deterministic l2 robustness, but the experimental evaluation lacks empirical attack validation and statistical reporting. Furthermore, ambiguities regarding the training dynamics of the last layer normalization and a direct contradiction in the regularization hyperparameter must be resolved before the claims can be trusted.

Revision targets:

- Clarify whether LLN normalization is enforced during training or applied only at inference, and adjust the optimization procedure or theoretical justification accordingly.

- Correct the conflicting values for gamma in Section 7 and the table captions, and detail the hyperparameter tuning protocol.

- Add empirical robustness results against standard adaptive attack suites (e.g., AutoAttack) to complement the certified accuracy metrics.

- Report mean and standard deviation of all accuracy metrics over multiple random seeds to establish statistical significance.

- Include a computational efficiency analysis comparing training time, inference latency, and certification overhead against the baseline methods.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in normalized logit definition
comments.quote: g_{i}(\mathbf{x})=\frac{\left(\mathbf{W}_{i,:}\right)^{T}f(\mathbf{x})}{\|\mathbf{W}_{i,:}\|_{2}}+\mathbf{b}_{i}
comments.explanation: This equation is mathematically undefined. Earlier in Section 4, the authors define $f(\mathbf{x})$ as the final logit vector in $\mathbb{R}^k$, while $\mathbf{W}_{i,:}$ is a row vector in $\mathbb{R}^m$ with $m > k$. The dot product $\mathbf{W}_{i,:}^T f(\mathbf{x})$ cannot be computed. The intended operation is the projection of the penultimate layer activations, which the authors previously denoted as $g(\mathbf{x})$. Replace $f(\mathbf{x})$ with $g(\mathbf{x})$ in both the numerator and the subsequent definition of $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ to fix the dimensional inconsistency.

comments.title: Cited numerical gains do not match table data
comments.quote: Similar to what we observe for CIFAR-100 in Table 1, the gains in provable robust accuracy ($\rho=108/255$) are significantly higher for deeper networks: LipConvnet-35 ($3.65\%$) and LipConvnet-40 ($4.35\%$) with decent gains in standard accuracy ($1.71$ and $1.61\%$ respectively).
comments.explanation: The percentages quoted here do not belong to CIFAR-100 or Table 1. They exactly match the provable robust accuracy gains for the +HH row in Table 2 (CIFAR-10): LipConvnet-35 gains $30.85\% - 27.20\% = 3.65\%$, and LipConvnet-40 gains $28.48\% - 24.13\% = 4.35\%$. The standard accuracy gains also match Table 2 ($1.71\%$ and $1.61\%$). This is a copy-paste error that misrepresents the CIFAR-100 results. Update the text to report the correct figures from Table 1, or correct the table if the text values are intended.

comments.title: Incorrect isolation of CR's effect on standard accuracy
comments.quote: Because LLN significantly improves the standard accuracy, we compare the standard accuracy numbers between rows "+ CR" and "+ LLN" to evaluate the drop due to CR.
comments.explanation: This comparison does not isolate the impact of Certificate Regularization. The "+ LLN" row uses the MaxMin activation, while the "+ CR" row uses the $\sigma_\theta$ activation alongside LLN and CR. Any difference in standard accuracy between these two rows conflates the activation change with the regularization effect. To accurately evaluate the drop caused by CR, compare the "+ CR" row against the "+ HH" row, which shares the same $\sigma_\theta$ activation and LLN but lacks CR. Revise the analysis to reflect this.

comments.title: Ambiguity in perturbation radius norm specification
comments.quote: We compare the provable robust accuracy using 3 different values of the $l_{2}$ perturbation radius $\rho=36/255,\ 72/255,\ 108/255$.
comments.explanation: The values $36/255$, $72/255$, and $108/255$ are standard $l_\infty$ perturbation budgets in the adversarial robustness literature. If these values are intended to represent $l_2$ radii, they are unusually small relative to typical $l_2$ budgets for CIFAR, and the distinction from $l_\infty$ needs explicit clarification. Readers will likely assume these are $l_\infty$ values based on convention. Rewrite the sentence to explicitly state the norm enforced by the certification code, or adjust the values to match standard $l_2$ budgets, because mixing up $l_2$ and $l_\infty$ radii fundamentally changes the adversarial threat model.

comments.title: Unjustified $\sqrt{2}$ factor in robustness bound
comments.quote: Using Theorem 7 in *Li et al. (2019b)*, we can derive the robustness certificate (in the $l_{2}$ norm) as $\mathcal{M}_{f}(\mathbf{x})/\sqrt{2}$.
comments.explanation: The standard Lipschitz-based lower bound for the $L_2$ distance to the decision boundary is $\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x})/(2L)$, where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant. The paper claims a tighter bound with a $\sqrt{2}$ denominator instead of $2$. While the authors cite Theorem 7 from Li et al. (2019b), the derivation relies on specific assumptions (e.g., linearity of the decision boundary, specific activation properties, or norm conversions) that are not stated here. Add a brief remark or footnote explaining the conditions under which the $\sqrt{2}$ factor applies, or verify that the cited theorem's assumptions are satisfied by the proposed network construction.

comments.title: Overstated distinction from randomized smoothing
comments.quote: In contrast, the defense we propose is deterministic and hence not comparable to randomized smoothing.
comments.explanation: Dismissing randomized smoothing as incomparable due to its probabilistic nature is a weak positioning claim. Both deterministic Lipschitz-based certification and randomized smoothing are standard baselines for evaluating certified robustness, and they are routinely compared in terms of certified radius, computational overhead, and scalability. The authors should acknowledge that while the certification mechanisms differ, the resulting guarantees and empirical performance are directly comparable. A fair comparison would discuss how their deterministic approach trades off certification tightness and inference speed against probabilistic smoothing methods, rather than asserting they are not comparable.

comments.title: Ambiguous extension of 2D orthogonal matrices
comments.quote: The same rules are directly extended to higher order tensors.
comments.explanation: The text defines $\mathbf{J}^{+}(\theta)$ and $\mathbf{J}^{-}(\theta)$ strictly as $2\times 2$ matrices. Claiming that 'the same rules are directly extended to higher order tensors' is mathematically imprecise. In practice, these are typically embedded as block-diagonal matrices (e.g., $\text{diag}(\mathbf{J}^{\pm}(\theta), \mathbf{I}_{d-2})$) to act on $\mathbb{R}^d$. Without specifying the embedding strategy, it is unclear how these matrices compose with convolution layers or how their spectral properties are preserved in higher dimensions. Add a precise definition of the block-diagonal embedding or clarify how the $2\times 2$ construction generalizes to the input dimension $d$.

comments.title: Typo in decision boundary condition
comments.quote: \min_{i\neq l}\;\min_{f_{i}(\mathbf{x}^{*})=f_{i}(\mathbf{x}^{*})}\|\mathbf{x}^{*}-\mathbf{x}\|_{2}\;\geq\;\frac{\mathcal{M}_{f}(\mathbf{x})}{\sqrt{2}}
comments.explanation: The condition inside the minimization contains a typographical error: $f_{i}(\mathbf{x}^{*})=f_{i}(\mathbf{x}^{*})$ is a tautology and fails to define the decision boundary where class $i$'s logit equals the correct class $l$'s logit. This should read $f_{i}(\mathbf{x}^{*})=f_{l}(\mathbf{x}^{*})$, which is correctly stated in the immediately following equation (3). Rewrite the condition to match equation (3).

We construc
comments.explanation: The equation numbering is duplicated. The Jacobian matrices are labeled as equation (2), and the robustness certificate inequality is also labeled as (2), followed by a corrected version labeled (3). This creates ambiguity when referencing these results later in the paper. Rewrite the equation label (2) for the Jacobian matrices as (1) and renumber all subsequent equations sequentially.

comments.title: Ambiguity in margin definition and certificate sign
comments.quote: For wrongly classified inputs,  $\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x}) / \sqrt{2} < 0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{relu}(\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x}) / \sqrt{2}) = 0$ .
comments.explanation: The text refers to $\frac{\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x})}{\sqrt{2}}$ as the 'robustness certificate' earlier, but then states it can be negative for misclassified inputs. Standard robustness certificates are inherently non-negative lower bounds on the distance to the nearest decision boundary. If $\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x})$ represents a signed logit margin, it can indeed be negative, but calling it a 'certificate' is non-standard and potentially confusing. Clarify the exact definition of $\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x})$ and whether it corresponds to a signed margin or a non-negative certified radius.

comments.title: Sign inconsistency in initial regularization description
comments.quote: To address this limitation, we can simply subtract the certificate i.e  $-\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x}) / \sqrt{2}$  from the usual cross entropy loss function during training.
comments.explanation: Subtracting $-\frac{\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x})}{\sqrt{2}}$ is mathematically equivalent to adding $+\frac{\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x})}{\sqrt{2}}$. For misclassified inputs where the margin is negative, adding a negative term would decrease the total loss, effectively rewarding confident misclassifications. This directly contradicts the authors' empirical claim that the term 'excessively penalizes the network for the misclassified examples.' The intended initial formulation was likely to subtract $+\frac{\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x})}{\sqrt{2}}$. Rewrite the phrase to align the mathematical expression with the stated goal of maximizing the margin.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about cross-entropy gradient saturation
comments.quote: That is, once the cross entropy loss becomes small, the gradients will no longer try to further increase the margin  $\mathcal{M}_f(\mathbf{x})$  even though the network may have the capacity to learn bigger margins.
comments.explanation: The claim that cross-entropy gradients vanish for margin maximization once the loss is small is generally true, but it requires a brief justification or citation to be rigorous. Specifically, the gradient of the cross-entropy loss with respect to the logits is $\sigma_y - \sigma_{y'}$, which approaches zero as the correct logit dominates. However, the rate of decay depends on the margin's magnitude. The authors should explicitly show that $\text{grad}_{\mathbf{x}} L_{CE}$ scales inversely with the margin to justify why the gradient 'will no longer try to further increase the margin.' Adding a short derivation or citing a standard result on cross-entropy gradient saturation would strengthen this motivation.

comments.title: Non-standard terminology for convolution layer constraints
comments.quote: Using SOC, we achieve the same bound on the approximation error of an orthogonal Jacobian as achieved in *Singla and Feizi (2021)*
comments.explanation: Convolution layers are linear operators represented by unrolled weight matrices that are typically tall (more output channels/pixels than input). Such matrices cannot be orthogonal in the strict square-matrix sense; they can only be semi-orthogonal (having orthonormal rows or columns). Referring to them as an 'orthogonal Jacobian' is mathematically imprecise and may confuse readers familiar with linear algebra conventions. Rewrite 'approximation error of an orthogonal Jacobian' as 'deviation from semi-orthogonality' because the SOC method constrains the singular values of the unrolled weight matrix rather than enforcing square orthogonality.

comments.title: Ambiguous reporting of CIFAR-10 accuracy gain
comments.quote: On CIFAR-10, we achieve significant improvements over prior works in provable robust accuracy ($5.81\%$) with only a minor drop in standard accuracy ($-0.29\%$).
comments.explanation: The parenthetical (5.81%) is ambiguous. It is unclear whether this figure denotes an absolute accuracy value or a relative improvement over baselines. The preceding sentence explicitly uses the word gains to describe similar metrics, so readers will naturally expect the same convention here. Without clarification, the claim could be misread as an absolute accuracy of 5.81%, which would be unusually low for CIFAR-10 robust benchmarks. Rewrite the phrase as a gain of 5.81 percentage points in provable robust accuracy to align with standard reporting conventions and eliminate ambiguity.

comments.title: Overstated requirement for orthogonal Jacobians
comments.quote: training such networks requires each layer to have an orthogonal Jacobian matrix (for all inputs) to prevent the gradients from vanishing during backpropagation.
comments.explanation: The assertion that training 1-Lipschitz CNNs requires an orthogonal Jacobian is too strong and mathematically imprecise. A Jacobian with a spectral norm bounded near 1 but not strictly orthogonal can effectively preserve gradient magnitudes during backpropagation. Additionally, convolutional layers are inherently non-square, which makes the term orthogonal technically incorrect. Isometric or semi-orthogonal accurately describes the norm-preserving property needed for GNP layers. The authors should replace 'requires' with 'is optimized by' or 'benefits from' and specify that isometric constraints are sufficient for gradient preservation.

comments.title: Missing Lipschitz justification for normalized certificate
comments.quote: \min_{i\neq l}\frac{g_{l}(\mathbf{x})-g_{i}(\mathbf{x})}{\|\mathbf{W}_{l,:}^{(n)}-\mathbf{W}_{i,:}^{(n)}\|_{2}},\qquad\text{where }\forall j,\;\;\mathbf{W}_{j,:}^{(n)}=\frac{\mathbf{W}_{j,:}}{\|\mathbf{W}_{j,:}\|_{2}},\;g_{j}(\mathbf{x})=\left(\mathbf{W}_{j,:}^{(n)}\right)^{T}f(\mathbf{x})+\mathbf{b}_{j}
comments.explanation: The paper asserts the certificate formula for the normalized logits but omits the brief justification that the Lipschitz constant of each normalized logit $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ remains 1. Since $\|\mathbf{W}_{i,:}^{(n)}\|_2 = 1$ and the penultimate function $g(\mathbf{x})$ is 1-Lipschitz, the composition has Lipschitz constant $\|\mathbf{W}_{i,:}^{(n)}\|_2 \cdot 1 = 1$. Consequently, the difference $g_l(\mathbf{x}) - g_i(\mathbf{x})$ has Lipschitz constant $\|\mathbf{W}_{l,:}^{(n)} - \mathbf{W}_{i,:}^{(n)}\|_2$, directly justifying the denominator. Add a one-line derivation showing $\text{Lip}(g_i) = \|\mathbf{W}_{i,:}^{(n)}\|_2 \cdot \text{Lip}(g) = 1$ after the sentence introducing the certificate to justify the denominator.

comments.title: Misleading repository name
comments.quote: The code for reproducing all experiments in the paper is available at https://github.com/singlasahil14/SOC.
comments.explanation: The repository name `SOC` strongly implies it hosts the code for the 'Second Order Certificates' baseline rather than the proposed method. Readers expecting the implementation of Last Layer Normalization, Certificate Regularization, and Householder Activations will be confused. To fulfill the reproducibility claim, the authors should either rename the repository to reflect the paper's title or explicitly state which subdirectories contain the proposed code. Additionally, reproducibility requires a specific commit hash to guarantee the exact code used for the experiments is accessible.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes three concrete improvements to 1-Lipschitz CNNs for certified l2 robustness: Last Layer Normalization, Certificate Regularization, and Householder activations. The experimental results show notable gains on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, and the theoretical characterization of Householder activations is a clean contribution. However, the paper has several significant gaps in its evaluation methodology, missing ablations, and incomplete justification of key claims that limit its overall impact.

Overview issues:

- Tightness of the Lipschitz bound is not empirically validated: The paper claims the network's Lipschitz constant is bounded by 1.0001, but provides no empirical verification of this bound's tightness. For a paper whose core contribution is certified robustness, the reader needs to see a comparison between the certified bound and the empirical Lipschitz constant (e.g., via power iteration or random sampling). Without this, it is unclear whether the certificate is meaningfully tight or significantly overestimates the true robustness. The authors should add a figure or table showing the distribution of certified radii vs. empirical robust radii for a subset of test examples.

- Certificate Regularization lacks analysis of the regularization coefficient γ: The paper introduces a certificate regularization term with coefficient γ, but only reports results for γ=0.1 (and γ=0.5 in the training details). There is no ablation study showing how the standard accuracy / certified accuracy trade-off varies with γ. This is a critical omission because the regularizer directly penalizes the network for small margins, and the optimal γ likely depends on the architecture and dataset. The authors should provide a sweep over γ values (e.g., 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0) for at least one architecture on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100.

- The necessity of Householder transformations is overstated: Theorem 1 proves that if a GNP piecewise linear function changes from Q1 to Q2 along a hyperplane, then Q2 must equal Q1(I - 2vv^T) for continuity. This is a valid mathematical result, but the paper then claims this shows that "every GroupSort activation is a special case of the more general Householder activation function." However, GroupSort can sort groups of arbitrary size, not just size 2. The paper only shows equivalence for group size 2 (MaxMin). For larger groups, the Jacobian is a permutation matrix, which is orthogonal but not necessarily a Householder reflection. The claim should be narrowed to group size 2, or the authors should prove that any GroupSort activation can be expressed as a composition of Householder activations.

- Last Layer Normalization is not compared to simpler alternatives: The paper proposes Last Layer Normalization (LLN) as a way to avoid the orthogonality constraints on the last layer. However, a natural baseline would be to simply not constrain the last layer at all and use the certificate from Proposition 1 without normalization. The paper mentions that this leads to smaller certificates due to large row norms, but does not provide quantitative results for this unnormalized baseline. Without this comparison, it is impossible to assess whether the gains from LLN come from the normalization itself or from the relaxation of orthogonality constraints. The authors should include results for the unnormalized last layer (using Proposition 1 directly) in Table 1.

- No evaluation of the Householder activation's learned parameters: The Householder activation introduces learnable parameters θ (one per pair of channels). The paper initializes θ = π/2 (equivalent to MaxMin) but never reports what values θ converge to after training. This is important because if the learned θ values remain close to π/2, then the Householder activation offers little benefit over MaxMin, and the gains observed might be due to other factors (e.g., the additional parameters themselves). The authors should include a histogram or summary statistics of the learned θ values for at least one trained network.

- Missing ablation: contribution of each component individually: The experimental design in Tables 1 and 2 combines multiple modifications (LLN, HH, CR) in a sequential manner, but never isolates the effect of each component alone. For example, on CIFAR-100, the row "+ HH" includes both LLN and HH, so the gain attributed to HH is confounded with the gain from LLN. The paper should include an ablation where each component is added individually without the others (e.g., SOC + HH without LLN, SOC + CR without HH or LLN). This is standard practice for papers proposing multiple innovations.

- The certificate regularization uses relu, which is not differentiable at zero: Equation (5) uses relu(M_f(x)/√2) as the regularization term. While relu is subdifferentiable, its gradient is zero for negative inputs and one for positive inputs, with a discontinuity at zero. This means that for inputs with a very small positive margin, the gradient of the regularizer is 1, but for inputs with a negative margin (misclassified), the gradient is 0. This abrupt transition could cause optimization difficulties. The authors should discuss whether they considered a smooth approximation (e.g., softplus) and why relu was chosen.

- Missing empirical verification of Lipschitz bound tightness: The paper claims the network's Lipschitz constant is bounded by 1.0001, but provides no empirical verification of this bound's tightness. For a paper whose core contribution is certified robustness, the reader needs to see a comparison between the certified bound and the empirical Lipschitz constant (e.g., via power iteration or random sampling). Without this, it is unclear whether the certificate is meaningfully tight or significantly overestimates the true robustness. The authors should add a figure or table showing the distribution of certified radii vs. empirical robust radii for a subset of test examples.

- Certificate Regularization lacks ablation on γ: The paper introduces a certificate regularization term with coefficient γ, but only reports results for γ=0.1 (and γ=0.5 in the training details). There is no ablation study showing how the standard accuracy / certified accuracy trade-off varies with γ. This is a critical omission because the regularizer directly penalizes the network for small margins, and the optimal γ likely depends on the architecture and dataset. The authors should provide a sweep over γ values (e.g., 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0) for at least one architecture on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add an empirical validation of the Lipschitz bound tightness (e.g., compare certified radii to empirical robust radii on a subset of test examples).

- Include an ablation study for the regularization coefficient γ, showing the trade-off between standard accuracy and certified accuracy.

- Provide an ablation study that isolates the effect of each proposed component (LLN, HH, CR) individually, without combining them.

- Report the learned values of the Householder activation parameters θ after training to demonstrate that they deviate from the MaxMin initialization.

- Add a baseline comparison to the unnormalized last layer (Proposition 1 without normalization) to quantify the benefit of LLN.

comments.title: Missing CIFAR-10 results table
comments.quote: Table 2, for each architecture, the row "SOC + $\mathrm{MaxMin}$" uses the $\mathrm{MaxMin}$ activation, the row "+ HH" uses
comments.explanation: The text repeatedly references Table 2 and describes its contents, but the only table provided in the paper is Table 1 (CIFAR-100 results). Table 2, which should contain the CIFAR-10 results, is missing. The reader cannot verify any of the numerical claims (gains of 3.65%, 4.35%, drops of -0.56%, etc.) because the data is not present. This is a critical omission. The authors must include Table 2.

comments.title: Overclaim on GroupSort as special case of Householder
comments.quote: this characterization proves that every $\mathrm{GroupSort}$ activation is a special case of the more general Householder activation function
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 only characterizes a single change of Jacobian along a hyperplane. GroupSort with group size > 2 (e.g., sorting groups of 4) has a Jacobian that is a permutation matrix, which is orthogonal but not necessarily a single Householder reflection. The paper only explicitly connects to MaxMin (group size 2). The claim as stated is too broad and not supported by the proof. Rewrite as: 'Thus, this characterization proves that the MaxMin activation (GroupSort with group size 2) is a special case of the more general Householder activation function.'

comments.title: Unit norm constraint on last layer rows not enforced in certificate
comments.quote: To address this limitation, we normalize all rows to be of unit norm before computing the logits. While this still requires all the rows to be of unit norm, their directions are now allowed to change freely
comments.explanation: The paper introduces Last Layer Normalization (LLN) where rows of the last weight matrix are normalized to unit norm before computing logits. However, the certificate formula given earlier uses the unnormalized weight rows (W_{l,:} and W_{i,:}). If the rows are normalized before computing logits, the certificate should use the normalized rows, not the original W rows. The paper does not clarify this, which could lead to incorrect certificate computation. Specify that the certificate uses the normalized rows, i.e., replace W_{l,:} with W_{l,:}/||W_{l,:}||_2 in the formula.

0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}z_{1}\\
z_{2}\end{bmatrix}&\quad\text{if}\ \ z_{1}\sin\left(\theta/2\right)-z_{2}\cos\left(\theta/2\right)>0\\

As written, the equation is malformed and cannot be parsed correctly. Rewrite equation (8) with a proper cases environment where each branch is a complete mathematical expression, e.g., using the standard LaTeX pattern: \begin{cases} ... & \text{if } ... \\ ... & \text{if } ... \end{cases}.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of Levine and Feizi (2021)
comments.quote: While *Levine and Feizi (2021)* provide deterministic robustness certificates using randomized smoothing, their certificates are in the $l_{1}$ norm and not directly applicable for the $l_{2}$ threat model studied in this work.
comments.explanation: The claim that Levine and Feizi (2021) only provide l_1 certificates is inaccurate. That work actually provides deterministic certificates for the l_2 norm by derandomizing a Gaussian-smoothed classifier, alongside l_1 certificates from Laplace smoothing. The paper should either correct this statement to reflect that l_2 certificates exist but may rely on different assumptions, or remove the claim about l_1 norm applicability.

comments.title: Reproducibility section is too brief
comments.quote: The code for reproducing all experiments in the paper is available at https://github.com/singlasahil14/SOC.
comments.explanation: This section consists of a single sentence pointing to a GitHub repository. For a paper that introduces multiple novel components and reports state-of-the-art results, the reproducibility section should also list key hyperparameters, training configurations, hardware used, and any random seed settings. Without this information, readers cannot independently verify the results even with the code. The authors should expand this section to include a brief summary of the experimental setup (e.g., optimizer, learning rate schedule, batch size, number of epochs, data augmentation, and the range of hyperparameters searched).

comments.title: Unsupported claim about CR drop magnitude
comments.quote: We observe a small drop in standard accuracy ($-0.04\%,-0.11\%$) only for LipConvnet-5 and LipConvnet-15 networks.
comments.explanation: The text claims specific percentage drops (0.04% and 0.11%) for LipConvnet-5 and LipConvnet-15 without providing the underlying accuracy values from Table 1. Without these numbers, the reader cannot verify whether the differences are within the margin of random variation or are statistically significant. The authors should include the standard accuracy values for rows '+ LLN' and '+ CR' for these two architectures, along with standard deviations if available, to allow the reader to assess the magnitude and significance of the drop.

comments.quote:

By adding LLN (the row "+ LLN"), we observe gains in standard (min gain of $1.10\%$) and provable robust accuracy (min gain of $1.71\%$ at $\rho=36/255$) across all the LipConvnet architectures (g
comments.explanation: The phrase "min gain of 1.10%" is ambiguous: it could mean the smallest gain observed across architectures, or it could be misinterpreted as a gain of exactly 1.10% for some unspecified architecture. The authors should clarify by stating "minimum gain of 1.10% across architectures" to avoid confusion.

comments.quote:

By adding LLN (the row "+ LLN"), we observe gains in standard (min gain of $1.10\%$) and provable robust accuracy (min gain of $1.71\%$ at $\rho=36/255$) across all the LipConvnet architectures (g

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper presents improvements to deterministic L2 robustness in CNNs by introducing Last Layer Normalization (LLN), Certificate Regularization (CR), and Householder activation functions. While the theoretical contributions regarding GNP activation functions are sound, the experimental evaluation lacks depth. The paper claims significant gains but fails to demonstrate the practical impact of these methods on standard benchmarks or compare them against the most relevant baselines in the literature.

The paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by characterizing GNP piecewise linear functions and introducing a new certification procedure. However, the experimental section is underwhelming. The improvements in provable robust accuracy, while statistically significant, are marginal in absolute terms (e.g., +5.81% on CIFAR-10). The paper does not address the fundamental expressivity-robustness tradeoff, and the lack of comparison to state-of-the-art randomized smoothing methods or recent deterministic approaches like 'On the Scalability of Certified Adversarial Robustness' weakens the claims of practical significance.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient comparison with recent state-of-the-art baselines: The paper compares against SOC, BCOP, and Cayley (Singla and Feizi, 2021). However, the literature context highlights a recent NeurIPS 2024 paper titled 'On the Scalability of Certified Adversarial Robustness' which achieves state-of-the-art deterministic robustness certificates on CIFAR-10. The current paper does not include results from this or other recent works. Without this comparison, it is impossible to determine if the proposed methods represent a genuine advancement or merely incremental improvements over a dated baseline. The authors should add a comparison table with the latest deterministic methods and discuss the gap between their approach and randomized smoothing.

- Lack of analysis on the expressivity-robustness tradeoff: The paper acknowledges the expressivity-robustness tradeoff but does not analyze it in depth. The standard accuracy on CIFAR-100 remains low (around 47-48%), which raises questions about the fundamental limitations of 1-Lipschitz GNP networks. The paper should include a discussion on whether the introduced methods can overcome this tradeoff or if they simply shift the Pareto frontier. A theoretical analysis or empirical demonstration of the capacity of these networks to learn complex decision boundaries would strengthen the contribution.

- Missing practical feasibility and deployment analysis: The paper focuses on theoretical guarantees and CIFAR-scale experiments but does not discuss the practical feasibility of deploying these certified models. The certification procedure involves computing margins and distances, which could be computationally expensive for large networks. The authors should provide an analysis of the certification time and memory overhead compared to standard inference. Additionally, the paper should address the gap between theoretical L2 robustness and practical robustness under floating-point arithmetic, as noted in the literature context.

- Unclear motivation for LLN on CIFAR-10: The paper states that LLN provides significant improvements when the number of classes is large (CIFAR-100) but does not observe significant gains on CIFAR-10. However, the theoretical motivation for LLN (relaxing orthogonality constraints to prevent information forgetting) applies generally. The authors should provide a more detailed analysis of why LLN is less effective on CIFAR-10. Is it due to the smaller number of classes, or is there a specific failure mode in the optimization process? A case study or ablation study on the training dynamics with and without LLN would clarify this.

- Limited empirical demonstration of Householder activation benefits: While the paper introduces Householder activations and proves they are a superset of MaxMin, the empirical benefits are primarily shown through the combination of LLN and CR. It is unclear how much of the improvement can be attributed to the Householder activation itself versus the other two techniques. The authors should perform an ablation study isolating the Householder activation to demonstrate its specific contribution to accuracy and robustness.

- Missing concrete toy example for Last Layer Normalization mechanism: The paper argues that orthogonality constraints cause 'forgetting' when the number of classes is large, but this intuition is abstract. A concrete toy example would ground the theory. Consider a simple 2-class linear network where the optimal weight vectors are not orthogonal. Show explicitly how the orthogonal constraint prevents the network from moving toward this optimum, whereas LLN allows the update. This specific computation would demonstrate that the method solves a fundamental constraint problem rather than acting as a mere tuning trick.

- Gradient interpretability claims unverified: The introduction lists 'interpretable gradients' as a key benefit of Lipschitz constraints, yet the paper delivers no empirical evidence for this. To validate the claim, the authors should visualize or quantify the gradient behavior (such as norm distribution or sparsity) of the trained networks. Without this, the promise of 'interpretable' models remains unfulfilled, leaving a gap between the stated contributions and the delivered content.

- Certification complexity not discussed for large-scale deployment: The new certification formula involves minimizing over all other classes, which scales linearly with the number of classes. For ImageNet-scale models with $k=1000$, this adds significant overhead during certification. The paper should discuss the computational complexity of the certification procedure or propose approximations to make the method practical for large-scale deployment, as the current focus on CIFAR limits the assessment of scalability.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a comparison table with the latest deterministic robustness methods (e.g., 'On the Scalability of Certified Adversarial Robustness') and discuss the gap with randomized smoothing.

- Include an analysis of the certification time and memory overhead to assess practical feasibility.

- Provide a detailed discussion on the expressivity-robustness tradeoff, including a theoretical or empirical analysis of the network's capacity.

- Conduct an ablation study to isolate the contribution of the Householder activation function.

- Explain the lack of LLN benefits on CIFAR-10 with a detailed analysis of the optimization dynamics or class-specific performance.

comments.title: Abstract lacks specific baseline citations

comments.explanation: The abstract reports specific numerical gains without naming the prior work. Readers cannot verify these claims without knowing the baseline numbers. Please include a reference to the specific prior work being improved.

comments.title: Abstract lacks specific baseline citations
comments.quote: On CIFAR-10, we achieve significant improvements over prior works in provable robust accuracy ($5.81\%$) with only a minor drop in standard accuracy ($-0.29\%$).
comments.explanation: The abstract claims improvements on CIFAR-10 (5.81% gain) but omits the specific baseline or reference. This makes the claim unverifiable. Please include the baseline reference and values.

comments.title: General principle claim is not fully supported
comments.quote: which provides a general principle for designing piecewise linear GNP functions.
comments.explanation: The conclusion states that the Householder activation provides a 'general principle' for designing GNP functions. However, the paper only proves a necessary condition for continuity (Theorem 1). This distinction is important; the theorem characterizes valid transitions rather than prescribing a design strategy.

comments.quote: e subtract the certificate because we want to maximize the certificate valu
comments.explanation: The explanation for the minus sign in the regularization term is confusing. The equation subtracts the certificate, which penalizes small margins. Clarify that this subtraction encourages larger certificates by increasing the loss when the margin is small.

comments.explanation: The calculation of the Lipschitz constant bound is slightly imprecise. The approximation (1+x)^n ≈ 1+nx yields 1.0000968, which rounds to the stated value, but the text should explicitly use the approximation formula to justify the bound.
